label: Image generation, editing & design

node.label: Image generation, editing & design
node.definition: Image-generation systems synthesize or edit visual content from text, images, masks, layouts, identities, depth, or other conditioning signals.
node.why_it_matters: This system is an application boundary where technical capability, evidence, safety controls, economics, and user workflow must succeed together.
examples: workflows combining Image generation, editing & design with Diffusion & score-based models
workflows combining Image generation, editing & design with Normalizing, rectified & flow-matching models
workflows combining Image generation, editing & design with U-Nets & encoder–decoder convnets
limitations: End-to-end quality is bounded by the weakest data, model, tool, interface, policy, infrastructure, and human-oversight component. Its direct dependencies include Diffusion & score-based models, Normalizing, rectified & flow-matching models.

dependencies.rationale: Image generation, editing & design integrates Diffusion & score-based models as part of an end-to-end application boundary.
Image generation, editing & design integrates Normalizing, rectified & flow-matching models as part of an end-to-end application boundary.
Image generation, editing & design integrates U-Nets & encoder–decoder convnets as part of an end-to-end application boundary.
In this atlas graph, Image generation, editing & design has an `applies` dependency on Quantized inference & compressed deployment with `common` strength.
Image generation, editing & design integrates Generative adversarial networks as part of an end-to-end application boundary.
In this atlas graph, Image generation, editing & design has an `applies` dependency on Generation-time watermarking with `optional` strength.
In this atlas graph, Image generation, editing & design has an `applies` dependency on Content credentials, signing & provenance with `optional` strength.
Image generation, editing & design integrates Model servers & inference runtimes as part of an end-to-end application boundary.
